questions.question: Explain the difference between kernel mode and user mode in operating systems. How does this separation provide protection?
keyPoints: Kernel mode allows privileged operations and direct hardware access
User mode restricts direct hardware access for security
System calls provide controlled transition between modes
This separation prevents user programs from crashing or corrupting the system
Hardware enforces this separation using privilege levels/rings

questions.question: What are the main advantages and disadvantages of the monolithic kernel structure?
keyPoints: Advantage: High performance due to direct communication between components
Advantage: Simpler design with all services in single address space
Disadvantage: Single point of failure - kernel crash affects entire system
Disadvantage: Difficult to maintain and extend as all components are tightly coupled
Disadvantage: Adding new services requires kernel recompilation in traditional design

questions.question: Describe the microkernel architecture and explain why it provides better reliability than monolithic kernels.
keyPoints: Microkernel keeps only essential core functions in kernel space
Core functions: minimal IPC, basic scheduling, basic memory management
All other services (file system, device drivers, networking) run as user-space servers
If a server crashes, it can be restarted without crashing entire system
Better fault isolation and security through process separation
Trade-off: More overhead due to inter-process communication

questions.question: What is the purpose of system calls? List and explain any three common system calls.
keyPoints: System calls provide controlled interface between user programs and kernel
They allow user programs to request privileged operations
Examples: fork() - create new process; exec() - execute program; open()/read()/write() - file operations
Each system call goes through a specific trap mechanism to switch from user to kernel mode
System calls maintain system security and integrity by preventing direct hardware access

questions.question: Explain the layered approach to operating system design. What are its benefits and drawbacks?
keyPoints: OS is organized into hierarchical layers where each layer uses services from layer below
Each layer provides abstraction - upper layers don't need to know implementation details
Benefits: Easier to implement, debug, and modify each layer independently
Benefits: Provides clear interfaces and promotes modularity
Drawbacks: Layer overhead may reduce performance
Drawbacks: Difficult to define clear boundaries between layers

questions.question: What are Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs)? How do they improve the flexibility of modern operating systems?
keyPoints: LKMs are pieces of code that can be loaded into kernel at runtime without reboot
Examples: device drivers, file system modules, network protocols
Linux uses LKM framework for dynamic functionality addition
Benefits: Add/remove hardware support without system restart
Benefits: Extend kernel capabilities dynamically
Benefits: Reduces kernel size by loading modules only when needed

questions.question: Explain the client-server model in operating system architecture. How does it relate to modern distributed systems?
keyPoints: Client-server model treats OS services as servers that respond to client requests
Services run as separate processes, communicating via IPC
Kernel often acts as the server for fundamental services
Provides modularity - servers can run locally or on remote machines
Enables distributed computing where services can be provided over network
Modern systems use this for network services, file sharing, and cloud computing

questions.question: Compare and contrast the following operating system structures: (a) Monolithic (b) Microkernel (c) Hybrid. Give examples of operating systems using each structure and discuss their relative advantages.
keyPoints: Monolithic: All OS services in single kernel address space - examples: traditional Unix, early Linux. Advantages: high performance; Disadvantages: poor fault tolerance
Microkernel: Minimal kernel with services in user space - examples: MINIX 3, QNX. Advantages: reliability, security; Disadvantages: performance overhead from IPC
Hybrid: Combines approaches - examples: Windows NT, modern Linux, macOS. Advantages: balance of performance and modularity
Discuss trade-offs between performance, reliability, security, and maintainability
Modern OS designs often use hybrid approaches to get best of both worlds

questions.question: Explain the complete process of how a system call works in an operating system. Include the role of the API library, software interrupts/traps, and mode switching.
keyPoints: User program calls API function (e.g., read()) from standard library
Library function prepares parameters and executes TRAP or SYSENTER instruction
CPU switches from user mode to kernel (privileged) mode via interrupt
Kernel verifies parameters, checks permissions, and executes the service
Kernel performs requested operation (e.g., reading from disk buffer
Kernel prepares return value and switches back to user mode
Control returns to library function which returns to user program
Explain that this ensures controlled access and maintains system protection

questions.question: Describe the structure of a modern operating system with respect to its main components. Explain how these components interact to provide services to applications.
keyPoints: Process Manager: creates, schedules, and terminates processes
Memory Manager: allocates/deallocates memory, implements virtual memory
File System: provides organized storage and retrieval of data
Device Drivers: interface between kernel and hardware devices
Communication Subsystem: handles IPC and network communication
Explain that these components work together through kernel
System calls provide interface for applications to request services
Components coordinate through internal kernel mechanisms
Modern OS uses modular design with hierarchical organization

questions.question: What is virtual machine architecture? How does it differ from traditional OS structure? Explain the benefits of virtualization in modern computing environments.
keyPoints: Virtual machine creates multiple isolated virtual environments on single hardware
Each VM runs its own guest OS, believing it has dedicated hardware
Hypervisor/VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) manages hardware sharing
Differences: Multiple OSes can run simultaneously, hardware is abstracted at new level
Benefits: Server consolidation - run multiple servers on one physical machine
Benefits: Isolation and security - one VM crashing doesn't affect others
Benefits: Easy backup, migration, and cloning of entire systems
Benefits: Legacy software support by running older OS versions
Examples: VMware, Hyper-V, VirtualBox, KVM
